sections.a_description: 22 diseases that kill indie projects — organized by the phase where they strike. Learn to recognize the symptoms before they become terminal.
The diseases you carry as the person behind the project.

items.a_description: When the founder becomes the bottleneck. Every decision flows through one person, and the project can't breathe without them.

a_image.alt: Single hand gripping dozens of tangled puppet strings controlling many small marionettes at once, dramatic side lighting from above, dark background, shallow depth of field

items.a_description: The slow burn that turns passion into exhaustion. You're still shipping, but you stopped caring three months ago.

a_image.alt: Single burnt matchstick still faintly smoldering next to a row of fresh unlit matches, dark background, dramatic macro photography, warm ember glow against cold tones

items.a_description: You shipped it, people are using it, and you're still convinced someone will figure out you have no idea what you're doing.

a_image.alt: Cracked vintage mirror reflecting a distorted shadowy silhouette, fragments showing different uncertain expressions, dark moody room, dramatic side lighting, shallow depth of field

items.a_description: Researching, planning, reorganizing — anything but the one task that would actually move the project forward.

a_image.alt: Ornate hourglass with sand frozen mid-fall suspended in time, golden grains hanging motionless in the air, dark background, dramatic macro photography, warm side lighting

sections.a_description: The diseases that strike before a single line of code is written.

items.a_description: Trapped in an endless loop of research, comparison, and what-ifs. You know everything about the market — except how to start.

a_image.alt: Vintage compass with the needle spinning wildly in multiple directions at once, motion blur on the needle, dark wooden surface, dramatic overhead lighting, macro photography

items.a_description: Every week there's a better idea. The current project is 60% done but the new one feels 100% exciting.

a_image.alt: Magpie bird surrounded by scattered glittering jewels, coins, and metallic trinkets on a dark surface, some objects half-buried, dark moody atmosphere, dramatic spotlight from above

items.a_description: Building first, asking questions never. The product is finished — but nobody was ever asked if they wanted it.

a_image.alt: Person mid-leap over a wide gap in a crumbling stone bridge, far side barely visible in fog, dramatic perspective from below, dark stormy sky, cinematic lighting

sections.a_description: The diseases that infect your product while you're building it.

items.a_description: The product started simple. Now it has a dashboard, an API, dark mode, and a settings page with 47 toggles. Nobody asked for any of it.

a_image.alt: Overloaded Swiss army knife with too many absurd tools sticking out, dark background, feature bloat metaphor

items.a_description: The timeline was two months. Then someone said "while we're at it" and now it's month six with no end in sight.

a_image.alt: Simple wooden doorframe with dozens of progressively larger ornate frames layered around it expanding outward, dark room, dramatic spotlight from above, forced perspective

items.a_description: Your MVP has a microservice architecture, CI/CD pipeline, and 90% test coverage. It has zero users.

a_image.alt: Ornate golden jewel-encrusted hammer resting next to a single tiny simple nail on a dark wooden surface, dramatic side lighting, shallow depth of field

items.a_description: Everything feels urgent, nothing feels important. The backlog is a graveyard of half-started features and unranked ideas.

a_image.alt: Messy chess board with dozens of mismatched pieces crowding each square, some pieces knocked over, dark moody lighting, overhead view, shallow depth of field

sections.a_description: The diseases that kill products after you build them but before they grow.

items.a_description: You talked to five friends who said "that's cool." That's not validation — that's politeness.

a_image.alt: Large magnifying glass hovering over a crowd of small wooden figurines, one figurine glowing under the lens, dark background, dramatic spotlight, shallow depth of field

items.a_description: The market research says there's a gap. But a gap in the market doesn't mean there's a market in the gap.

a_image.alt: Compass spinning wildly on a dark map with question marks, market direction uncertainty metaphor

items.a_description: You have users, but they're not staying. You have revenue, but it's not growing. Something fits — but not quite.

a_image.alt: Puzzle piece floating above a puzzle board almost fitting but not quite, dark atmospheric lighting

items.a_description: Your product is free, freemium, or priced so low it signals "hobby project." Charging real money feels like a betrayal.

a_image.alt: Price tag with the number scratched out multiple times, pricing indecision, dark dramatic lighting

sections.a_description: The diseases that keep your product from ever reaching people.

items.a_description: The product is 95% ready. It's been 95% ready for four months. There's always one more thing before it's "really" ready.

a_image.alt: Finger hovering over a big red launch button trembling, dark dramatic close-up, launch anxiety

items.a_description: You built it. Nobody came. Because nobody knew. Zero marketing budget, zero audience, zero plan to change that.

a_image.alt: Beautiful product sitting alone in a dark empty warehouse, no distribution channels metaphor

items.a_description: Launch day arrives and the only visitor is your own browser. You skipped the part where you build an audience before building the product.

a_image.alt: Empty theater with a spotlight on an empty stage, no audience metaphor, dark moody atmosphere

items.a_description: Your MVP launch strategy is "post on Reddit and hope." That's not a strategy — that's a wish.

a_image.alt: Minimalist rocket made of cardboard and tape on a real launch pad, scrappy MVP launch metaphor, dark

sections.a_description: The diseases that kill projects slowly — or keep them undead.

items.a_description: Not alive, not dead. The domain is renewed, the server is running, and the last commit was eight months ago.

a_image.alt: Zombie hand reaching out of laptop screen in dark room, undead project metaphor, horror atmosphere

items.a_description: "We're still in beta" is the startup version of "it's not you, it's me." The product will never be ready because ready means accountable.

a_image.alt: Door with a forever under construction sign covered in cobwebs, dark abandoned corridor

items.a_description: The hardest question in indie building: is this a problem you can fix, or a project you need to walk away from?

a_image.alt: Fork in a dark road with one path leading to light and the other to darkness, decision crossroads
